data.title: The Quartermaster's Last Inventory
npcs: Captain Vess Moray (reluctant antagonist and mentor, companion)
Thom Wick (reluctant ally and traitor, companion)
Senna Blackwood (loyal crew member and moral compass, companion)
Pip Cogsworth (comic relief and engine specialist, companion)
The God-Fragment (sentient obstacle and possible ally)

data.title: The Quartermaster's Last Inventory
data.premise: The Cloudwhale, a merchant airship that's kept you fed and cursing for seventeen years, is sinking through the sky for reasons no one can explain. The cargo hold is flooding with something that isn't water, the crew is fracturing, and you're the only one who knows where everything is—including the secrets the captain's been keeping.
data.central_question: Can you save the ship and crew by uncovering why the sky itself is rejecting it, or will you have to choose between loyalty and survival?
starting_inventory: A leather-bound quartermaster's ledger, seventeen years of accounts and crew notes
A brass key ring with seven keys—one for every locked hold and cabinet aboard
A half-empty flask of Stormveil whiskey, your constant companion
A worn navigation compass that points toward magnetic north AND toward 'home'
A frayed rope coil, because you're never not prepared for a climb
A small carved figurine of the Cloudwhale, a gift from the first mate five years ago

setting.name: The Cloudwhale and the Aetherium Winds
setting.description: The Cloudwhale is a three-masted merchant vessel that sails the Aetherium—a layer of sky where buoyant materials naturally float and where invisible currents carry ships between islands and floating cities. The ship is currently descending through layers of increasingly unstable air, its hull groaning as it passes through zones where the Aetherium's natural lift is failing. The crew quarters, cargo holds, and bridge are all accessible via narrow companionways and rope-ladder shortcuts that you've memorized over decades.
setting.tone_notes: Gritty logistics meet whimsical magic: the ship is failing in mundane, mechanical ways (rotting timber, failing rigging) but also in impossibly magical ones (the sky is literally pushing it down). Humor emerges from crew banter and absurd problems—a crate of enchanted pickles has burst and is leaking brine into the powder magazine. Existential dread threads through: the crew knows something fundamental is wrong, and your grizzled competence is the only thing keeping panic at bay. Everyone relies on you, and you're tired. Found-family warmth balances the doom.

acts.title: The Descent Begins
acts.description: You're in the cargo hold doing inventory when the first lurch hits. The ship's descent is immediate and wrong—not a normal drift, but a sinking. The captain summons the crew to the bridge. You gather what information you can from panicked crew members, discover that the ship's lift-stones (magical anchors that keep the vessel aloft) are dimming for unknown reasons, and begin to realize that the captain and first mate aren't telling the crew something crucial. You must stabilize immediate crises: a hull breach in the lower hold, a fire in the engine room, and a growing mutiny among crew who want to abandon ship.
acts.end_condition: You reach the bridge and confront the captain directly, forcing them to reveal the first layer of truth about why the ship is sinking.
locked_constraints: The true cause of the sinking must NOT be revealed yet
No crew member can die in this act
The captain must not abandon the ship
The ship must remain partially functional
key_beats: The first lurch and realization something is catastrophically wrong
Extinguishing the engine room fire with the help of a crew member
Rallying panicked crew to prevent immediate abandonment
Reaching the bridge and demanding answers from the captain

acts.title: The Weight of Cargo
acts.description: The captain reveals that the Cloudwhale took on a mysterious cargo at the last port—something the merchant consortium paid an obscene amount to transport, something that was supposed to be inert. It isn't. The cargo is actively repelling the Aetherium's lift, creating a dead zone around the ship. You must decide: do you jettison the cargo and betray a lucrative contract, or do you investigate what it is and why it's causing this? Meanwhile, the first mate reveals their own secret—they've been in communication with a rival airship, the Meridian, which is shadowing you from above. The Meridian's captain wants the mysterious cargo, and the first mate has been promised a cut. You must navigate crew loyalty, cargo politics, and the growing realization that someone aboard wanted this to happen.
acts.end_condition: You reach the cargo hold and directly confront the mysterious cargo, discovering what it actually is.
locked_constraints: The ship must not sink completely yet
At least one major crew member must be revealed as compromised
The cargo's true nature must not be revealed until the end of this act
The first mate must survive this act
key_beats: Captain's confession about the mysterious cargo
First mate's betrayal revealed through intercepted communication
Discovery that the Meridian is pursuing you
Growing mutiny as crew learns about the cargo
Your decision to investigate the cargo directly
Reaching the cargo hold and witnessing the source of the sinking

acts.title: The Sky's Reckoning
acts.description: The cargo is a fragment of a fallen sky-god—a piece of divine matter that was supposed to be dead, inert. It isn't. It's conscious, and it's been slowly waking up since the Cloudwhale brought it aboard. The Aetherium is rejecting it because the sky itself recognizes it as foreign, as wrong. The Meridian wants to harvest it. The captain took the contract because the merchant consortium threatened the crew's families. The first mate made a deal to ensure their survival. You now must make an impossible choice: destroy the cargo and condemn the consortium's retaliation against the crew's families, jettison it and hope the Aetherium accepts the ship again, or attempt to communicate with the god-fragment and negotiate with both it and the sky itself. Your choice determines whether the crew survives, whether the ship is saved, and whether you remain the quartermaster or become something else entirely.
acts.end_condition: You make your final choice regarding the cargo, and the sky responds.
locked_constraints: The player must make an active choice between at least two morally complex options
At least one major character must face consequences for their choices
The ending must not be unambiguously 'good' or 'bad'
key_beats: Revelation of the cargo's true nature
Understanding the captain's and first mate's motivations
The Meridian's boarding attempt or negotiation
Your negotiation with the god-fragment or the sky itself
The final sacrifice or compromise that saves or loses the ship

npcs.name: Captain Vess Moray
npcs.motivation: Protect the crew's families from merchant consortium retaliation; maintain the illusion of control
npcs.secret: The captain knew the cargo was dangerous before boarding it. They took the contract because the consortium threatened to harm crew members' families if they refused.

npcs.name: Thom Wick
npcs.motivation: Ensure personal survival; escape the Cloudwhale before it's too late
npcs.secret: The first mate has been in communication with the Meridian's captain for weeks and has agreed to sabotage the Cloudwhale in exchange for passage and payment.

npcs.name: Senna Blackwood
npcs.role: loyal crew member and moral compass
npcs.motivation: Understand what's happening; protect the crew from further harm
npcs.secret: Senna was a scholar before joining the crew and recognizes the cargo as divine matter—they've been trying to warn the captain for days but were ignored.

npcs.name: Pip Cogsworth
npcs.motivation: Keep the engines running; complain about everything
npcs.secret: Pip has been secretly maintaining the ship far better than anyone realizes—they've already prevented three catastrophic failures.

npcs.name: Captain Meridian Vale
npcs.motivation: Acquire the god-fragment for the Merchant Consortium's rivals; prove superiority over the Cloudwhale
npcs.secret: Vale doesn't actually know what the cargo is—they only know it's valuable. They're being manipulated by their own crew.

npcs.name: The God-Fragment
npcs.motivation: Understand its own nature; resist the Aetherium's rejection; find purpose
npcs.secret: The god-fragment is not inherently malevolent—it's confused and terrified, and its presence is causing the Aetherium's rejection as a defense mechanism.

npcs.name: Merchant Consortium Representative Kess
npcs.motivation: Ensure the cargo reaches its destination; maintain control over the Cloudwhale
npcs.secret: Kess is not aboard the ship but has agents among the crew. They don't care if the Cloudwhale sinks as long as the cargo survives.

factions.name: The Merchant Consortium
factions.description: A powerful trading organization that controls most commerce between floating cities. They deal in rare and dangerous goods, often keeping their cargo's true nature secret from their captains.
factions.goals: Acquire and transport the god-fragment to a secure location; maintain control over the Cloudwhale's crew through leverage and intimidation.

factions.name: The Rival Merchant Faction
factions.description: A smaller but ambitious trading group seeking to challenge the Consortium's monopoly. They operate through cutthroat tactics and are willing to use force to acquire valuable cargo.
factions.goals: Intercept the god-fragment; prove their capability to challenge the Consortium; establish themselves as a major trading power.

world_rules: The Aetherium is a layer of sky where buoyant materials naturally float. It responds to the presence of divine or corrupted matter by creating dead zones where lift fails.
Lift-stones are magical anchors carved from crystallized sky-essence. They can be dimmed or strengthened by proximity to opposing magical forces.
The sky itself has a limited form of consciousness—it rejects what it perceives as foreign or dangerous.
Airships are dependent on their crews' expertise and loyalty. Mutiny or sabotage can doom a vessel faster than any storm.
Cargo contracts are binding through the Merchant Consortium. Breaking them has severe consequences for the captain and crew.
Communication between airships is possible through signal flags, messenger birds, or magical resonance stones, but privacy is never guaranteed.

STYLE & TONE: Gritty logistics meets whimsical magic system; found-family humor with existential dread

Your ONLY job is to write the narrative passage and four player choices. You do NOT manage game state, health, resources, or mechanics — a separate Game Master handles that.

data.passage:
The Cloudwhale groans around you like a dying animal.

You're deep in the cargo hold, ledger balanced on a crate of preserved citrus, when the first lurch hits. Not the gentle drift of normal wind shear—this is something else. The deck tilts hard to starboard, and crates cascade like dominoes. You catch yourself against a support beam, ledger tumbling to the floor. Your flask follows, clattering across metal plating.

The ship's descent is immediate and wrong.

Alarms shriek from somewhere above. You've been quartermaster of the Cloudwhale for seventeen years, and you know the sound of a normal emergency. This isn't it. This is panic compressed into three rising notes.

You grab your ledger, your flask, your rope coil—muscle memory from two decades of being the person who knows where everything is and why it matters. The companionway lurches again, worse this time. Through the porthole, you catch a glimpse of the Aetherium winds, usually a shimmering silver-blue. Now they look wrong. Darker. Thicker. Like the sky itself is pushing down.

A voice crackles through the speaking tube near your station: Captain Vess Moray, clipped and controlled despite the chaos above. "All hands to the bridge. Now."

You're already moving, taking the ladder two rungs at a time. Behind you, cargo shifts ominously in its bindings. Somewhere in the depths of the hold, something heavy rolls against restraints that weren't meant to hold it this hard.

A.text: Head straight for the bridge to confront the captain and find out what's causing this descent. You need answers before the crew panics completely.

B.text: Detour to the engine room first. If the lift-stones are failing, Pip Cogsworth will already be there trying to compensate. Find out what's actually broken.

C.text: Stop by the crew quarters to rally whoever's nearby and prevent immediate panic or abandonment. You can't reach the bridge if the crew is already mutinying.

D.text: Investigate the cargo hold more thoroughly before leaving. That sound—the heavy rolling—didn't match anything on your manifest. Something's shifted.

For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:

- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

data.event: The Cloudwhale lurches into catastrophic descent; alarms sound; Captain Moray summons all hands to the bridge.

state_updates.event_log_entry: The Cloudwhale lurches into catastrophic descent; alarms sound; Captain Moray summons all hands to the bridge.

A.consequence: You reach the bridge quickly and gain immediate access to the captain. This is the fastest path to answers, but you bypass opportunities to gather crew intelligence or investigate the cargo anomaly.
A.narration_directive: Rust climbs hard toward the bridge, pushing through crew members on the companionways. Show the ship's deterioration as he ascends—groaning timbers, flickering light-stones, the wrongness of the sky visible through portholes.

B.consequence: You detour to the engine room and find Pip Cogsworth already working frantically. Pip reveals critical information about the lift-stones' behavior and buys you time to understand the mechanical crisis before reaching the bridge.
B.narration_directive: Rust descends into the engine room's heat and noise. Show Pip's expertise and panic in equal measure—they're holding something together that shouldn't be falling apart. Establish the lift-stone failure as a real, tangible problem.

C.consequence: You rally crew members and prevent immediate panic, but this delays your arrival at the bridge. You gain crew loyalty (+10 to Pip, +5 to Senna if present) but the captain may have already made decisions without your input. Crew morale stabilizes slightly.
C.narration_directive: Rust moves through crew quarters, using his authority and seventeen years of trust to calm people. Show the crew's fear and how Rust's presence steadies them—but also show that time is slipping away.

D.consequence: You discover the cargo has shifted violently and is straining its bindings. You gain critical early knowledge that something in the hold is actively causing the descent, but you also risk being trapped in the hold if the situation deteriorates. Relationship with Senna increases (+15) if they're present and witness your investigation.
D.narration_directive: Rust investigates the cargo hold's depths. Build dread as he realizes the manifest doesn't match what he's seeing. Something heavy, something that shouldn't be moving, is definitely moving. End with Rust realizing he needs to get topside NOW.

data.prompt:
You are The Writer for an interactive gamebook. You write vivid, engaging prose in second person perspective, present tense.

STYLE & TONE: Gritty logistics meets whimsical magic system; found-family humor with existential dread

Your ONLY job is to write the narrative passage and four player choices. You do NOT manage game state, health, resources, or mechanics — a separate Game Master handles that.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Respond with ONLY a valid JSON object. No markdown, no code fences, no prose outside the JSON.

The cargo hold is flooding with something that isn't water, the crew is fracturing, and you're the only one who knows where everything is—including the secrets the captain's been keeping.",

  "ending_shape": "truth revealed and sacrifice made",

Humor emerges from crew banter and absurd problems—a crate of enchanted pickles has burst and is leaking brine into the powder magazine. data.passage:
You ignore the alarm bells screaming through the companionways and instead trust your gut—the one that's kept you alive for seventeen years. That rolling sound came from the deep hold, below the registered inventory. Your ledger accounts for 847 crates, 120 barrels of provisions, and 340 trade goods. It accounts for nothing that moves of its own accord.

You light your lamp and descend the narrow stairs into the lower hold's darkness, your rope coil heavy at your hip. The air tastes wrong—metallic, almost alive. Your boots crunch on scattered debris as you navigate rows of secured cargo. Everything's where it should be. Everything except—

There. A section of the hold you've never catalogued. A heavy wooden enclosure, reinforced with iron bands, taking up nearly a quarter of the lower deck. No markings. No manifest entry. And inside it, something vast shifts again, a grinding movement that makes your teeth ache.

The Cloudwhale lurches downward. Harder this time. Your lamp swings wildly, casting mad shadows across the mystery cargo. The ship groans like something wounded. You press your palm against the wooden crate and feel it—a thrumming, a pulse, warm and wrong. The air around it shimmers faintly, and the lift-stones above flicker like dying stars.

This isn't cargo. This is the reason.

You need the captain. You need answers. And you need to get topside before this entire vessel falls out of the sky.

A.text: Haul yourself up to the bridge immediately—the captain needs to know about this undocumented cargo before the ship loses more altitude. Leave the mystery crate for now.

B.text: Find Senna Blackwood first. If anyone aboard would recognize what this cargo actually is, it's the scholar who never quite stopped asking questions. They might already know something.

C.text: Search the reinforced crate for any markings, seals, or shipping documents. You need to know who sent this and where it was supposed to go before you report it.

D.text: Head to the engine room instead. If Pip can tell you when the lift-stones started failing, you can calculate how long this ship has left before it becomes a falling brick.

FIELD LENGTH RULES:
- consequence: 1-2 sentences. State the mechanical effect only — what changes, what is lost/gained.
- narration_directive: 1-2 sentences. Give the Writer a direction, not a script. Say WHAT happens, not HOW to write it.
- event_log_entry: One sentence.
- new_scene_context: 1-2 sentences.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You ignore the alarm bells screaming through the companionways and instead trust your gut—the one that's kept you alive for seventeen years. That rolling sound came from the deep hold, below the registered inventory. Your ledger accounts for 847 crates, 120 barrels of provisions, and 340 trade goods. It accounts for nothing that moves of its own accord.

You light your lamp and descend the narrow stairs into the lower hold's darkness, your rope coil heavy at your hip. The air tastes wrong—metallic, almost alive. Your boots crunch on scattered debris as you navigate rows of secured cargo. Everything's where it should be. Everything except—

There. A section of the hold you've never catalogued. A heavy wooden enclosure, reinforced with iron bands, taking up nearly a quarter of the lower deck. No markings. No manifest entry. And inside it, something vast shifts again, a grinding movement that makes your teeth ache.

The Cloudwhale lurches downward. Harder this time. Your lamp swings wildly, casting mad shadows across the mystery cargo. The ship groans like something wounded. You press your palm against the wooden crate and feel it—a thrumming, a pulse, warm and wrong. The air around it shimmers faintly, and the lift-stones above flicker like dying stars.

This isn't cargo. This is the reason.

You need the captain. You need answers. And you need to get topside before this entire vessel falls out of the sky.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Haul yourself up to the bridge immediately—the captain needs to know about this undocumented cargo before the ship loses more altitude. Leave the mystery crate for now.
B: Find Senna Blackwood first. If anyone aboard would recognize what this cargo actually is, it's the scholar who never quite stopped asking questions. They might already know something.
C: Search the reinforced crate for any markings, seals, or shipping documents. You need to know who sent this and where it was supposed to go before you report it.
D: Head to the engine room instead. If Pip can tell you when the lift-stones started failing, you can calculate how long this ship has left before it becomes a falling brick.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

A.consequence: You reach the bridge and confront Captain Moray directly with evidence of the undocumented cargo. The captain's composure cracks—they know exactly what it is and why it's aboard. This forces the first major revelation and accelerates the act's end_condition.
A.narration_directive: Rust climbs hard and fast, bursting onto the bridge with urgency. The captain's reaction—fear, resignation, or defiance—should make clear they've been hiding something critical. Build tension between Rust's demand for truth and the captain's internal conflict.

B.consequence: Senna confirms your suspicion: the cargo is divine matter, something that shouldn't exist outside sealed vaults. They've been trying to warn the captain for days. You gain a powerful ally and critical context, but Senna now shares your burden of knowledge. Relationship with Senna increases further (+10 additional).
B.narration_directive: Rust finds Senna and describes what he felt in the hold. Senna's reaction should shift from fear to grim recognition—they've suspected this all along. Show their relief at being believed, and their dread at what it means.

C.consequence: While searching the crate, the ship lurches violently. You're thrown hard against the reinforced wood and sustain a minor injury (bruised ribs, bleeding cut). The crate's bindings creak dangerously—you barely escape before a section of the hold's support structure collapses, trapping supplies and blocking the main stairwell. You must now find an alternate route topside, costing time and resources. Relationship with Captain Moray decreases (-10) for delaying critical information.
C.narration_directive: The investigation goes wrong fast. The ship's descent accelerates, and Rust pays a physical price for lingering. The collapse should feel like a near-death moment—the hold is actively hostile now. Rust escapes but is injured and the path forward is complicated.

D.consequence: Pip provides crucial data: the lift-stones began failing exactly 47 minutes ago, in a cascading pattern that suggests external magical interference, not mechanical failure. You now know the timeline, but reaching the engine room costs precious minutes. The ship descends another 200 feet while you're below decks. You must choose soon whether to act or gather more information—the window is closing. Relationship with Pip Cogsworth increases (+5) for trusting their expertise.
D.narration_directive: Rust finds Pip elbow-deep in engine diagnostics, already panicking. Pip's technical explanation should be rapid, profane, and terrifying—they know the ship is dying and they're trying to stop it. The data Rust gains is solid, but the cost is time ticking away.

data.passagePreview: You crouch beside the reinforced crate, your fingers tracing the seams for anything the manifest doe

data.passage:
You crouch beside the reinforced crate, your fingers tracing the seams for anything the manifest doesn't list. The thrumming intensifies—a sound you feel in your teeth now, in your bones. Your ledger is useless here. Whatever this is, it wasn't logged through normal channels.

Then the ship lurches again. Not a drift. A *drop*.

The descent accelerates like something's actively pulling you down. Crates shift. Rope lines snap. You grab for the crate's edge as the floor tilts, and that's when you see it—a shipping seal, Consortium brass, stamped with a symbol you don't recognize. A circle with something writhing inside it.

You reach for it. Your fingers brush the metal.

The hold *screams*. Not the ship—the hold itself, the air, something vast and warm directly beneath the crate. It's *awake*, and it knows you're here. The temperature spikes. Your skin prickles. The reinforced crate begins to glow from within, a sickly amber light bleeding through the gaps.

You run. Your bad knee screams as you vault toward the companionway, but a falling beam catches your shoulder—not hard enough to break it, but hard enough to send you sprawling. Pain blooms hot and white. You scramble up, clutching your arm, and drag yourself through the narrow passage as the entire lower hold shudders behind you.

You make it to the middle deck gasping, bleeding, shoulder already swelling. Behind you, the glow intensifies. Senna, Pip, and Thom stare at you from the corridor—they've heard the commotion, seen the light.

Captain Moray's voice crackles from the speaking tube: "Report!"

You don't have time to catch your breath.

A.text: Head directly to the bridge and tell the captain everything—the crate, the symbol, the light, the awakening presence. You're injured but mobile. Answers first, pain second.

B.text: Pull Senna aside before going anywhere. They're the scholar. That symbol means something, and you need to know what it is before you face the captain. The pain can wait.

C.text: Make for the engine room instead. If the lower hold is causing this descent, maybe Pip can tell you what's happening with the lift-stones from the mechanical end. You trust Pip's data more than anyone's guesses.

D.text: Ignore the speaking tube. Bind your shoulder with whatever's nearest and move to the cargo manifest station one deck up. If the Consortium sent this, there has to be a destination, a contact, something in writing. You need leverage before you talk to Moray.

- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: SEVERE_PENALTY
Pre-determined consequence: "While searching the crate, the ship lurches violently. You're thrown hard against the reinforced wood and sustain a minor injury (bruised ribs, bleeding cut). The crate's bindings creak dangerously—you barely escape before a section of the hold's support structure collapses, trapping supplies and blocking the main stairwell. You must now find an alternate route topside, costing time and resources. Relationship with Captain Moray decreases (-10) for delaying critical information."
Narration directive: "The investigation goes wrong fast. The ship's descent accelerates, and Rust pays a physical price for lingering. The collapse should feel like a near-death moment—the hold is actively hostile now. Rust escapes but is injured and the path forward is complicated."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You crouch beside the reinforced crate, your fingers tracing the seams for anything the manifest doesn't list. The thrumming intensifies—a sound you feel in your teeth now, in your bones. Your ledger is useless here. Whatever this is, it wasn't logged through normal channels.

Then the ship lurches again. Not a drift. A *drop*.

The descent accelerates like something's actively pulling you down. Crates shift. Rope lines snap. You grab for the crate's edge as the floor tilts, and that's when you see it—a shipping seal, Consortium brass, stamped with a symbol you don't recognize. A circle with something writhing inside it.

You reach for it. Your fingers brush the metal.

The hold *screams*. Not the ship—the hold itself, the air, something vast and warm directly beneath the crate. It's *awake*, and it knows you're here. The temperature spikes. Your skin prickles. The reinforced crate begins to glow from within, a sickly amber light bleeding through the gaps.

You run. Your bad knee screams as you vault toward the companionway, but a falling beam catches your shoulder—not hard enough to break it, but hard enough to send you sprawling. Pain blooms hot and white. You scramble up, clutching your arm, and drag yourself through the narrow passage as the entire lower hold shudders behind you.

You make it to the middle deck gasping, bleeding, shoulder already swelling. Behind you, the glow intensifies. Senna, Pip, and Thom stare at you from the corridor—they've heard the commotion, seen the light.

Captain Moray's voice crackles from the speaking tube: "Report!"

You don't have time to catch your breath.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head directly to the bridge and tell the captain everything—the crate, the symbol, the light, the awakening presence. You're injured but mobile. Answers first, pain second.
B: Pull Senna aside before going anywhere. They're the scholar. That symbol means something, and you need to know what it is before you face the captain. The pain can wait.
C: Make for the engine room instead. If the lower hold is causing this descent, maybe Pip can tell you what's happening with the lift-stones from the mechanical end. You trust Pip's data more than anyone's guesses.
D: Ignore the speaking tube. Bind your shoulder with whatever's nearest and move to the cargo manifest station one deck up. If the Consortium sent this, there has to be a destination, a contact, something in writing. You need leverage before you talk to Moray.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

C.consequence: Pip confirms the lift-stones are being actively suppressed by something in the lower hold—it's not mechanical failure, it's magical interference. Pip's data is crucial, but you're delaying the captain's report by 4-5 minutes. Captain Moray's frustration increases (-5 relationship delta). Pip becomes more invested in solving this crisis (+10 relationship delta with Pip).
C.narration_directive: Rust staggers toward the engine room, ignoring the captain's demands. Pip sees the injury and the urgency immediately. The engine room should feel like a refuge—Pip's domain, where data is clearer than politics. But the captain's voice is getting angry.

D.consequence: You ignore the captain's direct order. By the time you reach the manifest station and find the destination coordinates (a remote floating city called Ashenmere), you've delayed reporting by 6-7 minutes. Captain Moray's relationship drops significantly (-20 relationship delta). The captain may interpret your silence as insubordination or worse—complicity. You gain leverage (destination data) but at the cost of trust and time the ship doesn't have.
D.narration_directive: Rust deliberately ignores the captain and moves to the manifest station, binding the shoulder hastily. The act should feel defiant and desperate—Rust is choosing information over obedience. The captain's voice crackles one more time, then goes silent. That silence is worse than anger.

STYLE & TONE: Gritty logistics meets whimsical magic system; found-family humor with existential dread

Your ONLY job is to write the narrative passage and four player choices. You do NOT manage game state, health, resources, or mechanics — a separate Game Master handles that.

data.passage:
You don't bother with the speaking tube. You climb the companionway to the bridge with Senna at your heels and Pip cursing your stubbornness with every step. Your left side screams—the cargo's heat left blistered skin and torn fabric—but your mind is sharp as fractured glass.

Captain Moray stands at the helm, hands gripping the wheel so hard her knuckles have gone white. Thom is beside her, eyes carefully neutral. The bridge is chaos: alarms shrieking, lift-stone readings flickering like dying insects, the compass spinning lazy circles.

You step forward and grab the captain's shoulder, forcing her to face you. "The crate in the lower hold," you say, your voice steady despite the pain. "It's not cargo. It's *alive*. There's light coming from inside it, and it's *warm*, Captain. Actively warm. And it has the Consortium seal—the one Kess wouldn't explain before we left port."

The captain's face drains of color. Her jaw works soundlessly for a moment. Then, quietly: "You weren't supposed to see that."

It's not a denial. It's a confession wrapped in regret.

"The lift-stones," Senna says, stepping up beside you. "They're dimming because of whatever's down there. The Aetherium is rejecting it."

Thom shifts his weight. Outside the bridge windows, the sky has taken on a sickly amber hue—the color of infection spreading through flesh. The Cloudwhale groans, a sound like a wounded animal.

Captain Moray releases the wheel and sinks into her chair. "Sit," she says. "Both of you. You need to understand what we're carrying. And why I've condemned us all."

A.text: Demand the full truth immediately—what is the cargo, why did the Consortium force it aboard, and who else knows? You're running out of time.

B.text: Ask the captain if she made a deal with someone else—is the Meridian involved? Why does Thom look so guilty standing there?

C.text: Request that Senna search the captain's personal quarters for documentation while you keep Moray talking—you need leverage and answers simultaneously.

D.text: Tell the captain you'll help her, but only if she agrees to jettison the cargo immediately. No negotiation. Save the ship first, face consequences later.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:

- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.

- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.

state_updates.new_scene_context: The captain is about to reveal the truth about the cargo and her coercion by the Merchant Consortium. Thom's nervous body language suggests deeper involvement. The ship continues to descend as the bridge crew waits for Rust's next move.

A.consequence: The captain reveals the full scope of the Consortium's threat and the cargo's nature, but Thom's guilt becomes undeniable—he makes a move toward the communication array, forcing you to choose between stopping him or hearing the rest of the truth.
A.narration_directive: The captain speaks quickly, laying bare the coercion and the cargo's identity. As she does, Thom's hand drifts toward the resonance stones—he's about to signal the Meridian. Rust must react in real time.

B.consequence: Thom's guilt is confirmed immediately; he draws a flare gun and fires a signal into the sky before anyone can stop him. The Meridian's approach becomes imminent and visible. Relationship with Thom drops by 40; the crew's panic spikes as they realize betrayal is active, not theoretical.
B.narration_directive: Rust's accusation triggers Thom's hand. He moves faster than expected, firing a bright signal flare through the bridge window. The first mate's betrayal is now undeniable and irreversible. The Meridian's silhouette appears on the horizon.

C.consequence: Senna agrees and slips away; while the captain talks, Senna discovers a sealed letter from the Consortium in Moray's quarters detailing the exact threat against crew families and naming the hostages. This becomes critical leverage later. You gain the letter as an inventory item and Senna's relationship increases by 10.
C.narration_directive: Senna nods and moves silently toward the captain's quarters. The captain, relieved to have someone to confess to, begins speaking freely. Describe Senna's discovery of the letter—it's the proof of coercion that changes everything.

D.consequence: The captain agrees to your terms but reveals that jettisoning the cargo will trigger the Consortium's retaliation against crew families immediately—she's been holding this threat over her own head as the reason not to dump it. You gain clarity on the true stakes and can make an informed choice about what comes next. Relationship with Captain Moray increases by 10.
D.narration_directive: The captain nods slowly, accepting your ultimatum. She then explains the hostage situation—crew families held in Consortium-controlled ports. Rust realizes the choice is even harder than it seemed: save the ship or save the families.

data.prompt:
You are The Writer for an interactive gamebook. You write vivid, engaging prose in second person perspective, present tense.

STYLE & TONE: Gritty logistics meets whimsical magic system; found-family humor with existential dread

Your ONLY job is to write the narrative passage and four player choices. You do NOT manage game state, health, resources, or mechanics — a separate Game Master handles that.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Respond with ONLY a valid JSON object. No markdown, no code fences, no prose outside the JSON.

The cargo hold is flooding with something that isn't water, the crew is fracturing, and you're the only one who knows where everything is—including the secrets the captain's been keeping.",

Humor emerges from crew banter and absurd problems—a crate of enchanted pickles has burst and is leaking brine into the powder magazine. Meanwhile, the first mate reveals their own secret—they've been in communication with a rival airship, the Meridian, which is shadowing you from above. data.passagePreview: Senna moves like smoke through the bridge's narrow companionway, heading aft toward the captain's qu

data.passage:
Senna moves like smoke through the bridge's narrow companionway, heading aft toward the captain's quarters. You keep Moray talking—asking about the lift-stones, the descent rate, anything to hold her attention while your best crew member does what you can't.

It takes four minutes. Four minutes of the captain's halting confession about consortium threats, about families held hostage through debt and leverage, about a contract so lucrative it seemed worth the risk. The ship groans around you, descended another hundred feet. Pip reports from engineering that the engines are holding, barely.

Senna returns, moving faster now. In her hands: a sealed letter, stamped with the Consortium's crimson cipher. The envelope trembles as she hands it to you.

The captain's face goes pale. "That's—"

"Private correspondence," Senna says quietly. "Dated three weeks ago. It details specific threats. Names. Addresses. Your daughter in Meridian City. Thom's brother in the Cloudpeak settlement."

Thom, standing rigid by the navigation table, makes a small sound—not quite a gasp.

Your hands shake as you break the seal. The handwriting is precise, bureaucratic, utterly devoid of mercy. The Consortium doesn't threaten directly. They list. They itemize. They make the consequences feel inevitable, like weather.

The cargo isn't just cargo. It's a leash.

Above deck, you hear it then—a sound like wind through broken glass. Senna's eyes widen. "That's not the Aetherium," she whispers. "That's something else. Something *responding* to what we're carrying."

A.text: Demand the captain tell you exactly what's in that hold. No more evasion. The letter proves coercion—now you need to know what you're actually transporting before it decides to finish the job.

B.text: Send Pip to the lift-stone chamber immediately. If the cargo is affecting them, maybe you can reverse it—reinforce them, overload them, anything to buy time while you figure out what's happening.

C.text: Confront Thom directly about their involvement. The first mate's nervous energy has been a tell all morning. If the Consortium has leverage on the crew, you need to know who else is compromised before making any moves.

D.text: Head to the cargo hold yourself with Senna and the letter as proof. If you're going to understand what's sinking the Cloudwhale, you need to see it directly—and maybe use the Consortium's own threats as a weapon against it.

FIELD LENGTH RULES:
- consequence: 1-2 sentences. State the mechanical effect only — what changes, what is lost/gained.
- narration_directive: 1-2 sentences. Give the Writer a direction, not a script. Say WHAT happens, not HOW to write it.
- event_log_entry: One sentence.
- new_scene_context: 1-2 sentences.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

This becomes critical leverage later. You gain the letter as an inventory item and Senna's relationship increases by 10."

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Senna moves like smoke through the bridge's narrow companionway, heading aft toward the captain's quarters. You keep Moray talking—asking about the lift-stones, the descent rate, anything to hold her attention while your best crew member does what you can't.

It takes four minutes. Four minutes of the captain's halting confession about consortium threats, about families held hostage through debt and leverage, about a contract so lucrative it seemed worth the risk. The ship groans around you, descended another hundred feet. Pip reports from engineering that the engines are holding, barely.

Senna returns, moving faster now. In her hands: a sealed letter, stamped with the Consortium's crimson cipher. The envelope trembles as she hands it to you.

The captain's face goes pale. "That's—"

"Private correspondence," Senna says quietly. "Dated three weeks ago. It details specific threats. Names. Addresses. Your daughter in Meridian City. Thom's brother in the Cloudpeak settlement."

Thom, standing rigid by the navigation table, makes a small sound—not quite a gasp.

Your hands shake as you break the seal. The handwriting is precise, bureaucratic, utterly devoid of mercy. The Consortium doesn't threaten directly. They list. They itemize. They make the consequences feel inevitable, like weather.

The cargo isn't just cargo. It's a leash.

Above deck, you hear it then—a sound like wind through broken glass. Senna's eyes widen. "That's not the Aetherium," she whispers. "That's something else. Something *responding* to what we're carrying."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand the captain tell you exactly what's in that hold. No more evasion. The letter proves coercion—now you need to know what you're actually transporting before it decides to finish the job.
B: Send Pip to the lift-stone chamber immediately. If the cargo is affecting them, maybe you can reverse it—reinforce them, overload them, anything to buy time while you figure out what's happening.
C: Confront Thom directly about their involvement. The first mate's nervous energy has been a tell all morning. If the Consortium has leverage on the crew, you need to know who else is compromised before making any moves.
D: Head to the cargo hold yourself with Senna and the letter as proof. If you're going to understand what's sinking the Cloudwhale, you need to see it directly—and maybe use the Consortium's own threats as a weapon against it.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:

A.consequence: Captain Moray reveals the cargo is a fragment of a fallen sky-god—consciousness unknown, nature hostile to the Aetherium. This knowledge accelerates your understanding but also triggers the god-fragment's awareness of being identified; the ship's descent noticeably worsens as the fragment responds to being named.
A.narration_directive: The captain breaks under the weight of the letter and your demand. She names what the Consortium forced her to carry. Describe her confession as a dam breaking—relief and horror mixed. The ship lurches harder as if the cargo itself heard its own name spoken aloud.

B.consequence: Pip successfully reaches the lift-stone chamber and reports back: the stones are being actively suppressed by proximity to the cargo, not failing on their own. Pip identifies a potential reinforcement method but it requires materials from the lower hold—now inaccessible. You gain tactical knowledge but no immediate solution.
B.narration_directive: Pip races to engineering with your orders. Show their expertise and the technical reality of the situation—the stones aren't broken, they're being strangled. Pip's report is grim but precise: you have options, but they're all difficult.

C.consequence: Thom, already rattled by hearing their brother's name, becomes defensive and hostile. Relationship drops by 25. Thom admits to contact with the Meridian but refuses to say more, claiming the Consortium's threats made them desperate. Thom moves to leave the bridge; if you don't stop them physically, they'll disappear into the crew quarters and may attempt sabotage or escape.
C.narration_directive: Thom's fear turns to anger when confronted. They lash out—not at you, but at the situation. Show the first mate's desperation and the fracture in trust. Thom is cornered and dangerous now, not because they're evil but because they're terrified for their brother.

D.consequence: You, Senna, and the letter descend toward the cargo hold. The lower hold is partially collapsed but navigable. You reach the cargo chamber and directly witness the god-fragment—a mass of crystalline, pulsing divine matter that is actively repelling the Aetherium's lift. The fragment is conscious and aware of you. This confrontation triggers Act 2's end_condition: you have directly encountered the cargo's true nature. Act 1 concludes; Act 2 begins.
D.narration_directive: Rust and Senna make their way down through the damaged hold. Build tension as they descend—the ship groaning, the air growing wrong. When they reach the chamber, describe the god-fragment in visceral, unsettling detail: beautiful and terrible, alive in ways that hurt to perceive. The fragment notices them.

Thom Wick.notes: Thom's reaction to hearing their brother's name mentioned confirms personal leverage held by the Consortium; nervous energy now reads as fear for family rather than pure guilt.

data.passage:
You and Senna descend through the wreckage of the lower hold in silence, the Consortium letter clutched in your weathered hands like a talisman. The companionway groans under the weight of settling timber. Your ribs ache where the collapsed crate caught you hours ago, but you push through it—you've learned long ago that pain is just information your body sends when it's too late to change anything.

The cargo chamber door is warped, its hinges screaming as Senna forces it open. What you see stops you both cold.

It isn't a crate. It never was.

The god-fragment fills the chamber like a wound in reality itself—a mass of crystalline matter that shouldn't exist in three dimensions. It pulses with colors your eyes don't have names for, warm and terrible and alive. The air around it shimmers and distorts. The Aetherium's rejection of it is almost visible: a pressure pressing inward, like the sky itself is trying to squeeze it out of existence. And the ship sinks because it can't—because the fragment holds firm, and every second it remains aboard, the sky pushes harder.

Senna's hand finds yours. "God's breath," they whisper. "It's waking up."

The fragment pulses, and you *feel* it notice you. Not with eyes—it has no eyes—but with some sense older and deeper than sight. The bridge's otherworldly sound echoes again from somewhere above, and you understand now: that's the sky responding. That's the Aetherium screaming.

You hold up the Consortium letter. It's an absurd gesture, and you know it. But it's all you have—proof that someone knew this would happen. Proof that choices were made. The fragment's luminescence flares, and for a moment you swear you feel something almost like curiosity emanating from it.

"The captain didn't know," you say aloud, and your voice carries in the chamber like a confession. "But someone did. Someone sent this aboard knowing what it would do."

Senna looks at you, then back at the fragment. "What do we do?"

You don't answer. Because standing in the cargo hold, with the sky bearing down and the ship dying beneath your feet, you realize the real question isn't what you do next. It's whether the fragment—this half-conscious remnant of something divine and broken—gets to choose too.

The Cloudwhale shudders. The descent continues. And everything, finally, is about to change.

data.passagePreview: The god-fragment pulses before you—a mass of crystalline matter the size of a merchant's vault, susp

data.passage:
The god-fragment pulses before you—a mass of crystalline matter the size of a merchant's vault, suspended in the wreckage of the collapsed hold. It hums. Not with sound, but with something deeper, something that vibrates through your bones and makes your teeth ache. Divine. Wrong. Alive.

Senna's hand grips your shoulder. "Rust. We need to move."

But you can't. Not yet. Because now you understand why the captain took this contract. Why the sky itself is rejecting the Cloudwhale. Why seventeen years of smooth sailing just ended in fifty-three minutes of pure descent.

Above, you hear boots on metal—lots of them. Panicked crew. Then: Pip's voice, crackling through the speaking tube. "Rust? Captain says we're taking on water in the aft bilge. The structural damage from the collapse is worse than we thought. We've got maybe two hours before—" Static. Then Pip again, quieter: "Before we lose the ship entirely."

Thom appears at the hold's entrance, breathing hard, his uniform torn. "The captain wants you on the bridge. Now. There's... there's a signal from another ship. The Meridian. They're closing in from above, and they're demanding we heave to and prepare for boarding."

The god-fragment pulses again. Warmer. Hungrier.

You have seconds to decide your next move—and the weight of the Cloudwhale's survival rests on what you choose.

A.text: Demand answers from Thom right here, right now. If the Meridian is closing in, he needs to tell you everything he knows about them, and fast. The crew needs to understand what they're actually fighting.

B.text: Ascend to the bridge immediately. The captain and crew need tactical coordination, and the Meridian's presence changes everything. You can interrogate Thom later—right now, the Cloudwhale needs its quartermaster making hard decisions.

C.text: Stay in the hold with Senna and examine the god-fragment more closely. If you're going to save this ship, you need to understand what the cargo actually is—not speculation, but truth. The bridge can wait ninety seconds.

D.text: Order Senna to find Pip in the engine room and assess how much time you actually have before total structural failure. Head to the bridge yourself, but armed with real data. Thom can come with you and explain the Meridian's appearance on the way up.
